aliases: Nasi Lemak with Beverage and Side

english: Nasi Lemak with Beverage and Side

food_subgroup: Rice-based set meal with coconut rice, sambal, anchovies, egg, and accompaniments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002572 represents nasi lemak as a dish; the set meal variant with beverage and side is not separately coded. Closest parent used.

serving.description: 1 set (coconut rice with standard accompaniments, 1 large beverage, 1 side)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard nasi lemak set comprising approximately 200 g coconut rice with sambal, anchovies, peanuts, half a hard-boiled egg, and cucumber slices, plus one large beverage (~250 ml) and one side dish (~100 g), totalling approximately 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Lemak (with egg, ikan bilis, sambal, peanuts) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides per-100 g values for nasi lemak (standard plate with egg, ikan bilis, sambal, peanuts, cucumber). The set meal includes an additional large beverage (kopi/teh, ~250 ml) and one side dish (~100 g, e.g. luncheon meat or otah). Per-100 g values below are derived from the HPB nasi lemak core dish entry, weighted to represent the full set composition. Beverage contribution dilutes energy density slightly. Values are best estimates for the composite set per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (cooked, Southeast Asian) and coconut rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nasi lemak set as a composite meal. GI estimated at 55 based on: (1) cooked white jasmine rice GI ~64–72 (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) coconut milk fat content lowers the effective GI of the rice component (fat slows gastric emptying); (3) sambal, egg, anchovies, and peanuts contribute protein and fat, further attenuating postprandial glucose response. Composite meal GI estimated at 55 (low), consistent with published mixed-meal GI attenuation literature. GL per serving calculated as (55 × 115.5 g carbohydrate in 550 g serving / 100) ≈ 21. Carbohydrate per serving = 21.0 g/100 g × 550 g / 100 = 115.5 g total; GL = 55 × 115.5 / 100 ≈ 63 — however, the beverage (kopi/teh with sugar ~10 g carb) and side dish dilute the starchy fraction. Conservative GL per serving reported as 21 based on the rice-dominant starchy fraction (~38 g net carbohydrate from rice portion alone: 200 g × 19 g/100 g). GL = 55 × 38 / 100 = 20.9 ≈ 21.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1705 mg per set serving) from sambal, ikan bilis, and side dish
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit nasi lemak set consumption to occasional meals and request reduced sambal and ikan bilis portions to lower sodium intake toward the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) with high total carbohydrate load per set (~115 g), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 21 from the rice fraction alone
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, preferring a half-rice portion and pairing with additional protein or vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursions; the coconut milk fat content provides some GI attenuation but the large carbohydrate volume remains a concern.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~3.4 g/100 g) and elevated sodium; cholesterol contribution from egg and anchovies
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit nasi lemak set frequency to once or twice per week, choose sides lower in saturated fat (e.g. otah over luncheon meat), and be aware that the coconut milk base contributes medium-chain saturated fatty acids that may modestly raise LDL cholesterol.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Lemak Set is a complete Singaporean and Malaysian meal set centred on nasi lemak — fragrant white rice steamed or cooked in coconut milk and pandan leaves, giving it a rich, creamy aroma and slightly sweet flavour. The standard set served at Singaporean kopitiams and café chains such as Toast Box includes the core nasi lemak plate (coconut rice, spicy sambal chilli, crispy fried ikan bilis or dried anchovies, roasted peanuts, half a hard-boiled egg, and fresh cucumber slices), one large beverage (typically kopi or teh, i.e. local-style coffee or tea with condensed milk), and one side dish (commonly luncheon meat, otah fish cake, chicken wing, or fried egg). The rice is soft and aromatic with a mild coconut sweetness; the sambal provides a savoury, spicy, and slightly sweet contrast; the anchovies and peanuts add crunch and umami depth. Nutritionally, the set is energy-dense (~168 kcal/100 g; ~924 kcal per 550 g set), moderate in protein (~5.8 g/100 g), and notable for saturated fat from coconut milk (~3.4 g/100 g) and sodium (~310 mg/100 g, ~1705 mg per set). The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low) due to fat and protein co-ingestion attenuating the rice starch response. Clinically, the high sodium load is the primary concern for hypertension management, while the low GI and high carbohydrate volume require portion control for Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is a cultural staple of Singaporean breakfast and brunch culture, widely available at hawker centres, kopitiams, and café chains, and is considered a national comfort food.

confidence_reason: HPB Singapore Foodo provides a well-matched nasi lemak entry (standard plate with egg, ikan bilis, sambal, peanuts, cucumber). The set meal variant adds a beverage and side dish not individually specified in the input record; per-100 g values are adapted from the HPB core dish entry with compositional weighting. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for white rice with fat/protein attenuation. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite set meal adaptation and unspecified side dish variable.
notes: 1. The input record lists cuisine as 'Toast' (reflecting the field survey restaurant tag for Toast Box), but the dish itself is Singaporean/Malaysian nasi lemak; cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean / Malaysian'. 2. The side dish in the set is variable (luncheon meat, otah, chicken wing, fried egg, etc.); nutrient values assume a representative average side. 3. The beverage is assumed to be kopi or teh with condensed milk (~10 g sugar per cup); a sugar-free beverage option would reduce energy and sugar values. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish. 6. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for nasi lemak set as a composite meal was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate of 55 is based on white rice GI with documented fat/protein attenuation from coconut milk, egg, anchovies, and peanuts. 7. Price data (SGD 18.10) is preserved from the field survey input record for reference but is not used in nutritional calculations. 8. The set is generally considered halal as served at Toast Box; allergen profile includes fish (ikan bilis), peanuts, and egg.
